Yaml

---
title: "Chapter A6: Accept–Reject Sampling for Dispersion in Gamma Regression"
author: "Kjell Nygren"
date: "`r Sys.Date()`"
output: rmarkdown::html_vignette
vignette: >
  %\VignetteIndexEntry{Chapter A6: Accept–Reject Sampling for Dispersion in Gamma Regression}
  %\VignetteEngine{knitr::rmarkdown}
  %\VignetteEncoding{UTF-8}
---

---
title: "Chapter A6: Accept-Reject Sampling for Dispersion in Gamma Regression"
author: "Kjell Nygren"
date: "`r Sys.Date()`"
output: rmarkdown::html_vignette
vignette: >
  %\VignetteIndexEntry{Chapter A6: Accept-Reject Sampling for Dispersion in Gamma Regression}
  %\VignetteEngine{knitr::rmarkdown}
  %\VignetteEncoding{UTF-8}
---

\`\`\`{r, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(
  collapse = TRUE,
  comment = "#>"
)
\`\`\`





# 1. Introduction 

This vignette explains the derivation of the posterior for the dispersion parameter
in a Gamma GLM with log link, and the construction of the surrogate function
`testfunc` used for rejection sampling in `rGamma_reg`.

We consider a Gamma GLM with log link, where the mean is

\[
\mu_i = \exp(x_i^\top \beta + \alpha_i),
\]

and the dispersion parameter is \(\phi > 0\).  
It is convenient to work with the **precision**

\[
v = \frac{1}{\phi}.
\]

Throughout this chapter, we treat the regression coefficients \(\beta\) and offsets \(\alpha_i\) as fixed and known.

## 1.2 Likelihood

We use the standard Gamma parameterization

\[
Y_i \mid v \sim \mathrm{Gamma}(\text{shape}=v,\ \text{rate}=v/\mu_i),
\]

so that

\[
\mathbb{E}(Y_i)=\mu_i,
\qquad
\mathrm{Var}(Y_i)=\frac{\mu_i^2}{v}.
\]

The log‑likelihood contribution of observation \(i\) is

\[
\ell_i(v)
=
v\log v
-
v\log \mu_i
+
(v-1)\log y_i
-
\log\Gamma(v)
-
\frac{v}{\mu_i} y_i.
\]

With weights \(w_i\), the total log‑likelihood is

\[
\ell(v)
=
\sum_{i=1}^n
w_i
\left[
v\log v
-
v\log \mu_i
+
(v-1)\log y_i
-
\log\Gamma(v)
-
\frac{v}{\mu_i} y_i
\right].
\]

## 1.3 Prior

We place a Gamma prior on the precision:

\[
v \sim \mathrm{Gamma}(a_0, b_0),
\qquad
\log p(v)
= a_0 \log b_0 \;-\; \log\Gamma(a_0)
\;+\; (a_0 - 1)\log v \;-\; b_0 v.
\]

## 1.4. Posterior Log‑Density

The posterior log‑density is

\[
f(v)
=
\ell(v)
+
\log p(v)
\;-\; \log (C_{1})
\]

where \( C_{1}\) is a normalizing constant for the posterior density.


# 2. The sampler


To build effective envelopes for the Gamma family with a Gamma prior for the precision, it is important to
be able to center the proposal close to the center of the posterior. To enable this, we introduce the following proposal
distribution and associated bounding function and establish a key claims.

## 2.1 Proposal Distribution

Let \( \bar{c}(v_{\text{tangent}}) \) denote the gradient of \(-\ell(v)\) evaluated at the tangency point:
\[
\bar{c}(v_{\text{tangent}}) = -\ell'(v_{\text{tangent}}).
\]

We then define a gamma proposal distribution by:

\[
v \sim \mathrm{Gamma}_{\text{prop}}\!\left(a_0 + \tfrac{1}{2}\sum_i w_i,\; b_0 - \bar{c}(v_{\text{tangent}})\right).
\]

where the log of the proposal distribution specifically takes the form

\[
\begin{aligned}
\log p_{prop}(v) & = (a_0+  \frac{1}{2}\sum_i w_i )\log [ b_0 - \bar{c}(v_{\text{tangent}})] \;-\; \log\Gamma(a_0+  \frac{1}{2}\sum_i w_i ) \\[4pt]
&  \;+\; ((a_0+  \frac{1}{2}\sum_i w_i ) - 1)\log v \;-\; [ b_0 - \bar{c}(v_{\text{tangent}})] v.
\end{aligned}
\]
---

In the implementation, this proposal is used in truncated form on the interval 
\([v_{\min}, v_{\max}]\), with \(v_{\min}, v_{\max}\) derived from a curvature-based 
Gamma surrogate and optional dispersion bounds.

## 2.2 Bounding function

Let $v_{\min}$ be lower bound for the precision $v$. We can then define a bounding function \(\log h(\cdot)\) by

\[
\log h(v)
=
\ell(v)
-
\bigl[\ell(v_{\text{tangent}})
-
\bar{c}(v_{\text{tangent}})(v - v_{\text{tangent}})\bigr]
-
\frac{1}{2}\Bigl(\sum_i w_i\Bigr)\log\!\left(\frac{v}{v_{\min}}\right).
\]


It follows from the concavity of \(\ell(\cdot)\) and the fact that 
\(\log(v/v_{\min}) \ge 0\) for \(v \ge v_{\min}\) that 
\(\log h(v) \le 0\) for all \(v \ge v_{\min}\).

## 2.3 Equivalence Claim

Throughout this section, all densities are understood on the truncated domain 
\(v \in [v_{\min}, v_{\max}]\), with normalizing constants absorbed into \(C_1\) and \(C_2\).

**Claim 1:**  

The posterior log density $f(.)$ satisfies the property that 

\[ f(v)=\log p_{prop}(v)+\log h(v) + C_{2} \]

where \[\begin{aligned}
C_{2} 
& = \Bigl( -  \log (C_{1}) \\[4pt]
& - [(a_0+  \frac{1}{2}\sum_i w_i ) \log\!\left(b_0 - \bar{c}(v_{\text{tangent}})\right) \;-\; \log\Gamma(a_0+  \frac{1}{2}\sum_i w_i )] \\[4pt]
& + [a_0 \log b_0 \;-\; \log\Gamma(a_0) ] \\[4pt]
& +[\ell(v_{\text{tangent}})+\bar{c}(v_{\text{tangent}})v_{\text{tangent}} ]\\[4pt]
& - [\frac{1}{2}\sum_i w_i] \log v_{\text{min}}\Bigr)\end{aligned}
\].

**Proof:**

\[
\begin{aligned}
f(v) & = \log p(v) +\ell(v) - \log (C_{1}) \\[4pt]
& = \Bigl( [a_0 \log b_0 \;-\; \log\Gamma(a_0)
\;+\; (a_0 - 1)\log v \;-\; b_0 v]  \\[4pt]
& + [[\frac{1}{2}\sum_i w_i] \log v - [-\bar{c}(v_{\text{tangent}}) v]   \\[4pt]
& + [(a_0+  \frac{1}{2}\sum_i w_i )\log [ b_0 - \bar{c}(v_{\text{tangent}})] \;-\; \log\Gamma(a_0+  \frac{1}{2}\sum_i w_i )] \\[4pt]
& - [a_0 \log b_0 \;-\; \log\Gamma(a_0) ] \Bigr) \\[4pt]
&   + \Bigl(\ell(v) -  [[\frac{1}{2}\sum_i w_i] \log v -[- \bar{c}(v_{\text{tangent}}) v]] \Bigr) \\[4pt]
& + \Bigl( -  \log (C_{1}) \\[4pt]
& - [(a_0+  \frac{1}{2}\sum_i w_i )\log [ b_0 - \bar{c}(v_{\text{tangent}})] \;-\; \log\Gamma(a_0+  \frac{1}{2}\sum_i w_i )] \\[4pt]
& + [a_0 \log b_0 \;-\; \log\Gamma(a_0) ] \Bigr) \\[4pt]
& = \Bigl( [(a_0+  \frac{1}{2}\sum_i w_i )\log [ b_0 - \bar{c}(v_{\text{tangent}})] \;-\; \log\Gamma(a_0+  \frac{1}{2}\sum_i w_i )] \\[4pt]
& + ((a_0+  \frac{1}{2}\sum_i w_i ) - 1)\log v \;-\; [ b_0 - \bar{c}(v_{\text{tangent}})]v  \Bigr) \\[4pt]
&   + \Bigl(\ell(v) -  [[\frac{1}{2}\sum_i w_i] \log v -[- \bar{c}(v_{\text{tangent}}) v]] \\[4pt]
& -[\ell(v_{\text{tangent}})+\bar{c}(v_{\text{tangent}})v_{\text{tangent}} ]  \\[4pt]
& + [\frac{1}{2}\sum_i w_i] \log v_{\text{min}} \Bigr) \\[4pt]
& + \Bigl( -  \log (C_{1}) \\[4pt]
& - [(a_0+  \frac{1}{2}\sum_i w_i )\log [ b_0 - \bar{c}(v_{\text{tangent}})] \;-\; \log\Gamma(a_0+  \frac{1}{2}\sum_i w_i )] \\[4pt]
& + [a_0 \log b_0 \;-\; \log\Gamma(a_0) ] \\[4pt]
& +[\ell(v_{\text{tangent}})+\bar{c}(v_{\text{tangent}})v_{\text{tangent}} ]\\[4pt]
& - [\frac{1}{2}\sum_i w_i] \log v_{\text{min}} \Bigr) \\[4pt]
& = \log p_{prop}(v) \\[4pt]
&   + \Bigl([\ell(v)-[\ell(v_{\text{tangent}})-\bar{c}(v_{\text{tangent}})(v - v_{\text{tangent}})]] \\[4pt]
& - \frac{1}{2}\Bigl(\sum_i w_i\Bigr)\log\!\left(\frac{v}{v_{\min}}\right) \Bigr) \\[4pt]
& + \Bigl( -  \log (C_{1}) \\[4pt]
& - [(a_0+  \frac{1}{2}\sum_i w_i )\log [ b_0 - \bar{c}(v_{\text{tangent}})] \;-\; \log\Gamma(a_0+  \frac{1}{2}\sum_i w_i )] \\[4pt]
& + [a_0 \log b_0 \;-\; \log\Gamma(a_0) ] \\[4pt]
& +[\ell(v_{\text{tangent}})+\bar{c}(v_{\text{tangent}})v_{\text{tangent}} ]\\[4pt]
& - [\frac{1}{2}\sum_i w_i] \log v_{\text{min}}\Bigr) \\[4pt]
& = \log p_{prop}(v) + \log h(v) + C_{2}
\end{aligned}
\]


## 2.4 Implementation Outline

To implement the sampler, we proceed as follows:

1. **Find the posterior mode** \(v_\star\) using a Gamma‑surrogate fixed‑point iteration.
2. **Approximate the posterior mean** \(v_{\text{mean}}\) using curvature at \(v_\star\) and a
   Gamma surrogate \(\Gamma(\alpha_{\!bar}, \beta_{\!bar})\).
3. **Set the tangency point** to
   \[
   v_{\text{tangent}} = v_{\text{mean}}.
   \]
4. **Build the proposal and bounding function** using \(v_{\text{tangent}}\) as above.
5. **Sample** as follows:
   - (a) Generate a candidate \(v_{\text{cand}}\) from the (possibly truncated) proposal
     \(\mathrm{Gamma}(\text{shape}_\text{prop}, \text{rate}_\text{prop})\).
   - (b) Draw \(u \sim \mathrm{Uniform}(0,1)\).
   - (c) Accept if
     \[
     \log h(v_{\text{cand}}) - \log u > 0.
     \]

The accepted draws for \(v\) are then transformed to dispersion by \(\phi = 1/v\).






## 4.1 Proxy Model and Shape Adjustment

**Step 1 (proxy model).**  
We build an equivalent “proxy model” with the following three key properties:

1. **Shape shift.**  
   \[
   \text{shape}_\text{prop} = \text{shape}_\text{prior} + \frac{1}{2}\sum_i w_i.
   \]

2. **Proxy log‑likelihood.**  
   \[
   \ell^{*}(v) = \ell(v) - \frac{1}{2}\Bigl(\sum_i w_i\Bigr)\log v,
   \]
   where \(\ell(v)\) is the original weighted log‑likelihood for the precision \(v\).

3. **Concavity preserved.**  
   The proxy log‑likelihood \(\ell^{*}(\cdot)\) is constructed so that it **retains the concavity** of the
   original log‑likelihood in \(v\). This ensures that tangent envelopes based on \(\ell^{*}\) produce valid
   upper bounds for the posterior log‑density.

The purpose of this step is to allow a **shape‑shifted Gamma proposal** whose shape parameter incorporates
\(\tfrac{1}{2}\sum_i w_i\), while working with a proxy log‑likelihood that is still concave in \(v\).
This makes it possible to construct tight envelopes with proposals that are centered near the posterior.

---

## 4.2 Tangency, Proposal, and Bounding Function

**Step 2 (tangency and linear shift).**  
We pick one or more tangency points, and for each component of the grid (if more than one), we **shift a
linear term** from the proxy model into the rate of the Gamma proposal.

In the case of a single tangency, it is best to use a tangency point at the **posterior mean**. Generally,
denote by \(\bar{c}(v)\) a gradient for \(-\ell(v)\) at \(v\). Then we build a proposal and a bounding
function \(\log h(\cdot)\) as follows.

### Gamma proposal

We define a Gamma proposal with

- **shape:**
  \[
  \text{shape}_\text{prop}
  =
  \text{shape}_\text{prior} + \frac{1}{2}\sum_i w_i,
  \]

- **rate:**
  \[
  \text{rate}_\text{prop}
  =
  \text{rate}_\text{prior} - \bar{c}(v_{\text{tangent}}),
  \]

where
\[
-\bar{c}(v_{\text{tangent}})
=
\sum_i w_i
\Bigl[
\log(v_{\text{tangent}})
+ 1
- \log(\mu_i)
+ \log(y_i)
- \psi(v_{\text{tangent}})
- \frac{y_i}{\mu_i}
\Bigr],
\]
and \(\psi(\cdot)\) is the digamma function.


### Bounding function

The bounding function \(\log h(\cdot)\) is defined by
\[
\log h(v)
=
\ell(v)
-
\bigl[\ell(v_{\text{tangent}})
+
\ell'(v_{\text{tangent}})(v - v_{\text{tangent}})\bigr]
-
\frac{1}{2}\Bigl(\sum_i w_i\Bigr)\log\!\left(\frac{v}{v_{\min}}\right),
\]

where \(\bar{c}(v_{\text{tangent}}) = -\ell'(v_{\text{tangent}})\) and \(v_{\min}>0\) is a lower bound
derived from the curvature‑based Gamma surrogate and optional dispersion bounds.

Because \(\ell(\cdot)\) is concave and the penalty term
\(-\tfrac{1}{2}(\sum_i w_i)\log(v / v_{\min})\) is non‑positive for \(v \ge v_{\min}\), we have
\[
\log h(v) \le 0
\quad\text{for all } v \ge v_{\min},
\]
which is the key inequality needed for a valid accept–reject envelope.

---

## 4.3 Implementation Outline

To implement the sampler, we proceed as follows:

1. **Find the posterior mode** \(v_\star\) using a Gamma‑surrogate fixed‑point iteration.
2. **Approximate the posterior mean** \(v_{\text{mean}}\) using curvature at \(v_\star\) and a
   Gamma surrogate \(\Gamma(\alpha_{\!bar}, \beta_{\!bar})\).
3. **Set the tangency point** to
   \[
   v_{\text{tangent}} = v_{\text{mean}}.
   \]
4. **Build the proposal and bounding function** using \(v_{\text{tangent}}\) as above.
5. **Sample** as follows:
   - (a) Generate a candidate \(v_{\text{cand}}\) from the (possibly truncated) proposal
     \(\mathrm{Gamma}(\text{shape}_\text{prop}, \text{rate}_\text{prop})\).
   - (b) Draw \(u \sim \mathrm{Uniform}(0,1)\).
   - (c) Accept if
     \[
     \log h(v_{\text{cand}}) - \log u > 0.
     \]

The accepted draws for \(v\) are then transformed to dispersion by \(\phi = 1/v\).


### 4.   



### 
To construct an envelope for rejection sampling, we rewrite the posterior as

\[
f(v)
=
(\text{shape}_2 - 1)\log v
-
\text{rate}_1\, v
+
\text{testfunc}(v)
+
\text{const},
\]

where

\[
\text{shape}_2 = a_0 ,
\]

\[
\text{rate}_1
=
b_0
+
\sum_i w_i\left(
\frac{y_i}{\mu_i}
-
\log\frac{y_i}{\mu_i}
-
1
\right),
\]

and the concave remainder is

\[
\text{testfunc}(v)
=
-\sum_i w_i
\left[
\log\Gamma(v)
+
v
-
v\log v
\right].
\]

This decomposition isolates the part of the posterior that will be utilized in the accept/reject procedure.

### 4. Tangent‑Envelope Construction

Let

\[
v^{*} = \frac{\text{shape}_2}{\text{rate}_1}
\]

be the mode of the Gamma surrogate.  
Define

\[
g^{*} = \left.\frac{d}{dv}\text{testfunc}(v)\right|_{v=v^{*}},
\qquad
\text{rate}_2 = \text{rate}_1 - g^{*}.
\]


**Claim**

Define a proposal distribution by  
\[
v_{\text{prop}} \sim \mathrm{Gamma}(\text{shape}_2,\ \text{rate}_2),
\]
where \(\text{rate}_2 = \text{rate}_1 - g^{*}\) and \(g^{*} = \text{testfunc}'(v^{*})\).

Then:

(i)
\[
\begin{aligned}
f(v) &= (\text{shape}_2 - 1)\log v - \text{rate}_2 v +
\text{const} \\[6pt]
&   +  \text{testfunc}(v) - \text{testfunc}(v^{*}) - g^{*}(v - v^{*}) \\[6pt]
&  +  \text{testfunc}(v^{*}) - g^{*}v^{*}
\end{aligned}
\]

and 

(ii)
\[
\begin{aligned}
\text{testfunc}(v)
&\;-\; \text{testfunc}(v^{*})
\;-\; g^{*}(v - v^{*})
\;\le\; 0.
\end{aligned}
\]

---

**Proof**

To see (i), note that  

\[
\begin{aligned}
f(v) &= (\text{shape}_2 - 1)\log v - \text{rate}_1 v + \text{testfunc}(v) + \text{const} \\[6pt]
&= (\text{shape}_2 - 1)\log v - (\text{rate}_2 + g^{*})\, v + \text{testfunc}(v) + \text{const} \\[6pt]
&= (\text{shape}_2 - 1)\log v - \text{rate}_2 v + \text{const} \\[6pt]
&   + \text{testfunc}(v) - \text{testfunc}(v^{*}) - g^{*}(v - v^{*}) \\[6pt]
&   +  \text{testfunc}(v^{*}) - g^{*}v^{*}
\end{aligned}
\]

where the first equality follows from the definition of the log‑posterior,  the second from the definition of  \( \text{rate}_2 \) and the third from adding and subtracting \(\text{testfunc}(v^{*}) - g^{*}v^{*}\).

To see (ii), we first show that \(\text{testfunc}\) is concave.

Recall
\[
\text{testfunc}(v)
=
-\sum_i w_i\Big[
\log\Gamma(v)
+
v
-
v\log v
\Big],
\qquad v>0,\ w_i \ge 0.
\]

The first derivative is
\[
\begin{aligned}
\text{testfunc}'(v)
&=
-\sum_i w_i
\Big[
\frac{d}{dv}\log\Gamma(v)
+
\frac{d}{dv}\{v - v\log v\}
\Big] \\[4pt]
&=
-\sum_i w_i
\Big[
\psi(v)
-
\log v
\Big],
\end{aligned}
\]
where \(\psi(v) = \dfrac{d}{dv}\log\Gamma(v)\) is the digamma function.

The second derivative is
\[
\begin{aligned}
\text{testfunc}''(v)
&=
-\sum_i w_i
\Big[
\psi'(v)
-
\frac{1}{v}
\Big] \\[4pt]
&=
\sum_i w_i
\Big[
-\psi'(v)
+
\frac{1}{v}
\Big].
\end{aligned}
\]

The derivative \(\psi'(v)\) is the trigamma function \(\psi_1(v)\), which admits the series representation
\[
\psi_1(v)
=
\sum_{n=0}^{\infty}\frac{1}{(v+n)^2},
\qquad v>0.
\]
Hence \(\psi_1(v) = \psi'(v) > 0\) for all \(v>0\), and in fact
\[
\psi_1(v)
=
\sum_{n=0}^{\infty}\frac{1}{(v+n)^2}
\;\ge\;
\frac{1}{v}.
\]

Therefore, for each \(v>0\),
\[
-\psi'(v)
+
\frac{1}{v}
\le
 0
\]
Hence this yields
\[
\text{testfunc}''(v) \le 0,
\]
so \(\text{testfunc}\) is concave on the domain under consideration.

By concavity, for any \(v, v^{*}\) in this domain,
\[
\text{testfunc}(v)
\le
\text{testfunc}(v^{*})
+
\text{testfunc}'(v^{*})(v - v^{*}).
\]
With
\[
g^{*} = \text{testfunc}'(v^{*}),
\]
this becomes
\[
\text{testfunc}(v)
-
\Big[
\text{testfunc}(v^{*})
+
g^{*}(v - v^{*})
\Big]
\le 0,
\]
which establishes property (ii).

**Claim**

Define a proposal distribution by
\[
v_{\text{prop}} \sim \mathrm{Gamma}(\text{shape}_2,\ \text{rate}_2).
\]
Then

(i) The normalized posterior density satisfies
\[
\pi(v)
=
\frac{1}{Z'}
\,q(v)\,
\exp\{R(v)\},
\]
where
\[
q(v)
=
\frac{\text{rate}_2^{\,\text{shape}_2}}{\Gamma(\text{shape}_2)}
v^{\text{shape}_2 - 1}
\exp(-\text{rate}_2 v),
\]
and
\[
R(v)
=
\text{testfunc}(v)
-
\Big[
\text{testfunc}(v^{*})
+
g^{*}(v - v^{*})
\Big],
\qquad
g^{*} = \text{testfunc}'(v^{*}),
\quad
\text{rate}_2 = \text{rate}_1 - g^{*}.
\]

(ii) The remainder term is always nonpositive:
\[
R(v) \le 0,
\qquad
\forall v > 0.
\]

---

**Proof**

We start from the log-posterior representation
\[
f(v)
=
(\text{shape}_2 - 1)\log v
-
\text{rate}_1\, v
+
\text{testfunc}(v),
\]
so that
\[
\pi(v)
=
\frac{1}{Z}\exp\{f(v)\}.
\]

*Part (i).* Substitute \(\text{rate}_1 = \text{rate}_2 + g^{*}\) into \(f(v)\):
\[
\begin{aligned}
f(v)
&=
(\text{shape}_2 - 1)\log v
-
(\text{rate}_2 + g^{*}) v
+
\text{testfunc}(v) \\
&=
(\text{shape}_2 - 1)\log v
-
\text{rate}_2 v
+
\big[\text{testfunc}(v) - g^{*} v\big].
\end{aligned}
\]

Add and subtract \(\text{testfunc}(v^{*}) - g^{*} v^{*}\) inside the bracket:
\[
\begin{aligned}
\text{testfunc}(v) - g^{*} v
&=
\Big[
\text{testfunc}(v)
-
\text{testfunc}(v^{*})
-
g^{*}(v - v^{*})
\Big]
+
\big[\text{testfunc}(v^{*}) - g^{*} v^{*}\big] \\
&=
R(v) + \text{testfunc}(v^{*}) - g^{*} v^{*}.
\end{aligned}
\]

Hence
\[
\begin{aligned}
f(v)
&=
(\text{shape}_2 - 1)\log v
-
\text{rate}_2 v
+
R(v)
+
\big[\text{testfunc}(v^{*}) - g^{*} v^{*}\big].
\end{aligned}
\]

Exponentiating,
\[
\exp\{f(v)\}
=
\exp\big\{\text{testfunc}(v^{*}) - g^{*} v^{*}\big\}
\,
v^{\text{shape}_2 - 1}
\exp(-\text{rate}_2 v)
\,
\exp\{R(v)\}.
\]

Thus
\[
\pi(v)
=
\frac{1}{Z}
\exp\{f(v)\}
=
\frac{1}{Z'}
v^{\text{shape}_2 - 1}
\exp(-\text{rate}_2 v)
\exp\{R(v)\},
\]
for some \(Z' > 0\). Recognizing the Gamma proposal density \(q(v)\) and absorbing constants into \(Z'\), we obtain
\[
\pi(v)
=
\frac{1}{Z'}\,q(v)\,\exp\{R(v)\},
\]
which proves (i).

*Part (ii).* Assume \(\text{testfunc}\) is concave and differentiable. By concavity, for any \(v, v^{*} > 0\),
\[
\text{testfunc}(v)
\le
\text{testfunc}(v^{*})
+
\text{testfunc}'(v^{*})(v - v^{*}).
\]

Using \(g^{*} = \text{testfunc}'(v^{*})\), this becomes
\[
\text{testfunc}(v)
\le
\text{testfunc}(v^{*})
+
g^{*}(v - v^{*}),
\]
so
\[
\text{testfunc}(v)
-
\Big[
\text{testfunc}(v^{*})
+
g^{*}(v - v^{*})
\Big]
\le 0,
\]
that is,
\[
R(v) \le 0,
\qquad \forall v > 0.
\]

If \(\text{testfunc}\) is strictly concave in a neighborhood of \(v^{*}\), then equality holds if and only if \(v = v^{*}\); otherwise \(R(v) < 0\). This proves (ii).

Claim:

Define a proposal distribution by

\[
v_{\text{prop}} \sim \mathrm{Gamma}(\text{shape}_2,\ \text{rate}_2).
\]

then

(i) [Insert equality showing that the posterior equals the decomposition into the log-proposal+ the remainer term]

(ii) The remainder terms is always <=0

Proof:

Show several equalities establishing 1. Demonstrated that \text{testfunc}(v) is a concave function and that this implies that the remainer terms hence is <=0
 


### Redundant

From the definitions, we can write \(\text{testfunc}(v)\) explicitly as the log‑likelihood plus two extra terms.

The weighted log‑likelihood is
\[
\ell(v)
=
\sum_{i=1}^n
w_i
\left[
v\log v
-
v\log \mu_i
+
(v-1)\log y_i
-
\log\Gamma(v)
-
\frac{v}{\mu_i} y_i
\right].
\]

The remainder term is
\[
\text{testfunc}(v)
=
\sum_{i=1}^n w_i
\left[
v\log v
-
(v-1)*1
- \log\Gamma(v)
- \frac{1}{2}\log v
-1
\right].
\]



The remainder term is
\[
\text{testfunc}(v)
=
- \sum_{i=1}^n w_i
*(\frac{1}{2}\log v)
+
\sum_{i=1}^n w_i
\left[
v\log v
-
(v-1)*1
- \log\Gamma(v)
-1
\right].
\]



Then
\[
\text{testfunc}(v)
=
\ell(v)
+
\text{term}_1(v)
+
\text{term}_2(v),
\]
where
\[
\text{term}_1(v)
=
\sum_{i=1}^n w_i\Big[-\tfrac{1}{2}\log v - v\Big]
=
-\Big(\tfrac{1}{2}\sum_{i=1}^n w_i\Big)\log v
-
\Big(\sum_{i=1}^n w_i\Big)v,
\]
and
\[
\text{term}_2(v)
=
\sum_{i=1}^n w_i
\left[
v\log \mu_i
-
(v-1)\log y_i
+
\frac{v}{\mu_i} y_i
\right].
\]

Equivalently, for each \(v>0\),
\[
\boxed{
\text{testfunc}(v)
=
\ell(v)
-
\Big(\tfrac{1}{2}\sum_i w_i\Big)\log v
-
\Big(\sum_i w_i\Big)v
+
\sum_i w_i
\left[
v\log \mu_i
-
(v-1)\log y_i
+
\frac{v}{\mu_i} y_i
\right].
}
\]







A proposed value is accepted if

\[
\log u
<
\text{testfunc}(v_{\text{prop}})
-
\left[
\text{testfunc}(v^{*})
+
g^{*}(v_{\text{prop}} - v{*})
\right],
\qquad
u \sim \mathrm{Uniform}(0,1).
\]

The final dispersion estimate is

\[
\phi = \frac{1}{v}.
\]





## Posterior Sampling for Dispersion in Gamma GLMs

This vignette explains the derivation of the posterior for the dispersion parameter
in a Gamma GLM with log link, and the construction of the surrogate function
`testfunc` used for rejection sampling in `rGamma_reg`.

### Model Setup

We assume observations \(y_i\) follow a Gamma distribution with fixed mean \(\mu_i\)
and unknown precision \(v\):

\[
y_i \mid v \sim \mathrm{Gamma}(\text{shape}=v,\ \text{rate}=v/\mu_i),
\qquad
\mu_i = \exp(x_i^\top \beta + \alpha_i).
\]

The log-likelihood for a single observation is

\[
\log f(y_i \mid v, \mu_i)
=
v \log\left(\frac{v}{\mu_i}\right)
- \log \Gamma(v)
+ (v - 1)\log y_i
- v \frac{y_i}{\mu_i}.
\]

With observation weights \(w_i\), the total log-likelihood is

\[
\ell_{\text{like}}(v)
=
\sum_i w_i \left[
v \log\left(\frac{v}{\mu_i}\right)
- \log \Gamma(v)
+ (v - 1)\log y_i
- v \frac{y_i}{\mu_i}
\right].
\]

### Prior

\[
v \sim \mathrm{Gamma}(a_0, b_0),
\qquad
\ell_{\text{prior}}(v) = (a_0 - 1)\log v - b_0 v.
\]

### Posterior Log-Density

\[
\ell(v)
=
\ell_{\text{like}}(v)
+
\ell_{\text{prior}}(v).
\]

To facilitate rejection sampling, rewrite the posterior as

\[
\ell(v)
=
(\text{shape2}-1)\log v
-
\text{rate1}\,v
+
\text{testfunc}(v)
+
\text{const},
\]

where

\[
\text{shape2} = a_0 + \frac{n}{2},
\qquad
\text{rate1} = b_0 + \sum_i w_i\left(\frac{y_i}{\mu_i} - \log\frac{y_i}{\mu_i} - 1\right).
\]


<!-- Copilot proposed this change removing 1 from rate1 expression-->
<!-- \text{shape2} = a_0 + \frac{n}{2}, -->
<!-- \qquad -->
<!-- \text{rate1} = b_0 + \sum_i w_i\left(\frac{y_i}{\mu_i} - \log\frac{y_i}{\mu_i}\right). -->

The surrogate curvature function is:

```{r}
testfunc <- function(v, wt) {
  -sum(
    lgamma(wt * v) +
    0.5 * log(wt * v) +
    wt * v -
    wt * v * log(wt * v)
  )
}
```

This function is concave in \(v\) and is used to construct a tangent envelope at

\[
v^\star = \frac{\text{shape2}}{\text{rate1}}.
\]

### Proposal Distribution

The rejection sampler draws proposals from

\[
v \sim \mathrm{Gamma}(\text{shape2},\ \text{rate2}),
\qquad
\text{rate2} = \text{rate1} - g^\star,
\]

where

\[
g^\star = -\frac{d}{dv}\,\text{testfunc}(v^\star).
\]

### Acceptance Probability

A proposed value \(v_{\text{prop}}\) is accepted if

\[
\log u
<
\text{testfunc}(v_{\text{prop}})
-
\left[
\text{testfunc}(v^\star)
+
g^\star (v_{\text{prop}} - v^\star)
\right],
\qquad
u \sim \mathrm{Uniform}(0,1).
\]

The final output is the dispersion parameter

\[
\phi = \frac{1}{v}.
\]

---

## Tangent-Envelope Sampler for Gamma Dispersion (Deviance based)

Let \(y_i > 0\) be observations with fixed means \(\mu_i > 0\) and weights \(w_i > 0\).
Let \(v > 0\) be the precision parameter with prior \(v \sim \mathrm{Gamma}(a_0, b_0)\).



shape2 <- a_0 + 0.5 * n
rate1  <- b_0 + sum(wt * (y / mu - log(y / mu) - 1))

<!-- Copilot proposed change -->
<!-- shape2 <- a_0 + 0.5 * n -->
<!-- rate1  <- b_0 + sum(wt * (y / mu - log(y / mu))) -->

Define the concave remainder:

testfunc <- function(v, wt) {
  -sum(
    lgamma(wt * v) +
    0.5 * log(wt * v) +
    wt * v -
    wt * v * log(wt * v)
  )
}

The posterior density satisfies

\[
\pi(v)
\propto
v^{\text{shape2}-1}
\exp(-\text{rate1}\,v)
\exp\{\text{testfunc}(v)\}.
\]

### Tangent-Envelope Sampler

Let \(v^\star > 0\) be an anchor point and

\[
g^\star = \text{testfunc}'(v^\star).
\]

Define

\[
\text{rate2} = \text{rate1} - g^\star,
\qquad
q(v) = \mathrm{Gamma}(\text{shape2},\ \text{rate2}).
\]

The rejection sampler:

1. Draw \(V \sim q(v)\).  
2. Accept \(V\) with probability  
   \[
   \alpha(V)
   =
   \exp\left[
     \text{testfunc}(V)
     -
     \text{testfunc}(v^\star)
     -
     g^\star (V - v^\star)
   \right].
   \]

---

## Accept-Reject Sampling for Dispersion in Gamma Regression

In Gamma regression models with known regression coefficients, the posterior
distribution for the dispersion parameter \(\phi\) is not conjugate.

### Model Setup

\[
y_i \sim \mathrm{Gamma}(\nu,\ \mu_i),
\qquad
\mu_i = \exp(x_i^\top \beta + \alpha_i),
\qquad
\nu = 1/\phi.
\]

The log-likelihood contribution is

\[
\log p(y_i \mid \nu)
\propto
\nu \log \nu
-
\nu \log\left(\frac{y_i}{\mu_i}\right)
-
\log \Gamma(\nu).
\]

Example code:

mu1 <- t(exp(alpha + x %*% b))

testfunc <- function(v, wt) {
  -sum(
    lgamma(wt * v) +
    0.5 * log(wt * v) +
    wt * v -
    wt * v * log(wt * v)
  )
}

cbar <- -sum(
  wt * digamma(wt * vstar) -
  wt * log(wt * vstar) +
  0.5 / vstar
)

### Posterior

With prior \(\nu \sim \mathrm{Gamma}(a_0, b_0)\),

\[
f(\nu)
=
(a_0 - 1)\log \nu
-
b_0 \nu
+
\sum_{i=1}^n
\left[
\nu \log \nu
-
\nu \log\left(\frac{y_i}{\mu_i}\right)
-
\log \Gamma(\nu)
\right]
+
\text{const}.
\]

### Envelope Construction

1. Find the posterior mode \(\nu^\star\) by solving \(f'(\nu)=0\).  
2. Compute \(f(\nu^\star)\) and \(f'(\nu^\star)\).  
3. Define  
   \[
   g(\nu)
   =
   f(\nu^\star)
   +
   f'(\nu^\star)(\nu - \nu^\star).
   \]

### Sampling Algorithm

Copilot Proposed change is second part

1. Propose  
   \[
\nu \sim \mathrm{Gamma}(a_1, b_1),
\qquad
a_1 = a_0 + \frac{n}{2},
\quad
b_1 = b_0 + \sum_i\left(\frac{y_i}{\mu_i} - \log\frac{y_i}{\mu_i} - 1\right),
\qquad
a_1 = a_0 + \frac{n}{2},
\quad
b_1 = b_0 + \sum_i\left(\frac{y_i}{\mu_i} - \log\frac{y_i}{\mu_i}\right).
\]
   
   

   
   
2. Accept if  
   \[
   f(\nu) - g(\nu) > \log U,
   \qquad
   U \sim \mathrm{Uniform}(0,1).
   \]
3. Return \(\phi = 1/\nu\).

---

## Extension to Quasi-Likelihood Models

Quasi-Poisson (\(\mathrm{Var}(Y)=\phi\mu\)) and quasi-binomial
(\(\mathrm{Var}(Y)=\phi\mu(1-\mu)\)) models lack a full likelihood.

To apply an accept-reject sampler, one must specify:

- a working likelihood (e.g., overdispersed Poisson or binomial), or  
- a pseudo-likelihood or empirical-Bayes surrogate.

Without such a surrogate, the method cannot be directly applied.


##  Old Theoretical Development

## Introduction

This Vignette extends the theory of the Nygren and Nygren (2006) paper to the case of gamma priors with log-concave likelihood functions. In section 1, we restate some of the basic theory and then state a key claim similar to that for Multivariate normal priors in that paper. 

## 1.0 General theory

## 1.1 Likelihood-Subgradient Densities and Envelope Construction

The accept-reject construction used in this chapter relies on factorizations of the form
\[
\pi(v \mid y) = a \, h(v) \, q(v),
\]
where \(q\) is a density from which we can sample directly, \(0 \le h(v) \le 1\), and \(a > 1\) is the normalizing constant that determines the acceptance rate. To motivate the specific envelopes used later, we summarize the key definitions from Nygren and Nygren (2006).

### Definition (Subgradient)
Let \(h : \mathcal{V} \to \mathbb{R}\) be a function defined on a convex set \(\mathcal{V}\).  
A number \(c(v_0)\) is a subgradient of \(h\) at \(v_0\) if
\[
h(v) \ge h(v_0) + c(v_0)\,(v - v_0)
\qquad \text{for all } v \in \mathcal{V}.
\]
If \(h\) is differentiable and convex, then \(c(v_0) = h'(v_0)\).

In our setting, the function of interest is the negative log of a bounding function for the likelihood.  
Let \(g(v)\) satisfy
\[
f(y \mid v) \le g(v) \qquad \text{for all } v>0.
\]
If the likelihood is log-concave in \(v\), then \(-\log g(v)\) is convex and admits a subgradient at every interior point.

### Definition (Generalized likelihood-subgradient density)
Let \(v_0 > 0\) be an anchor point, and let \(c(v_0)\) be a subgradient of \(-\log g(v)\) at \(v_0\).  
Given a prior density \(\pi(v)\), define the exponentially tilted density
\[
q(v)
=
\frac{\exp\{-c(v_0)\,v\}\,\pi(v)}
     {\operatorname{MGF}_{\pi}(-c(v_0))},
\qquad v>0,
\]
where
\[
\operatorname{MGF}_{\pi}(t)
=
\int_0^\infty e^{t v}\,\pi(v)\,dv.
\]
We call \(q\) the generalized likelihood-subgradient density at \(v_0\).

This construction has two key properties:

1. **Preservation of tractability.**  
   For exponential-family priors (including finite mixtures of Gamma distributions), exponential tilting preserves the family.  
   Each mixture component remains Gamma with the same shape and a shifted rate.  
   Thus sampling from \(q\) is straightforward.

2. **Envelope factorization.**  
   The posterior admits the factorization
   \[
   \pi(v \mid y)
   =
   a(v_0)\, h_{v_0}(v)\, q(v),
   \]
   where
   \[
   h_{v_0}(v)
   =
   \frac{\exp\{-c(v_0)(v - v_0)\}\, g(v)}{g(v_0)},
   \qquad 0 \le h_{v_0}(v) \le 1,
   \]
   and
   \[
   a(v_0)
   =
   \frac{g(v_0)\, \operatorname{MGF}_{\pi}(-c(v_0))}{f(y)}.
   \]
   The acceptance rate in an accept-reject sampler is \(1/a(v_0)\).

These definitions provide the foundation for Claim 1, where the prior is a finite mixture of Gamma distributions. In that case, exponential tilting yields closed-form expressions for the updated mixture weights and rate parameters, making the likelihood-subgradient construction especially convenient for the dispersion parameter \(v\) in Gamma regression.

**Claim (Gamma-mixture likelihood-subgradient density).**

Suppose the prior for the scalar precision parameter \(v>0\) is a finite mixture of 
Gamma distributions,
\[
\pi(v) = \sum_{i=1}^k p_i \,\pi_i(v),
\qquad
\pi_i(v)
= \frac{\beta_i^{\alpha_i}}{\Gamma(\alpha_i)} v^{\alpha_i - 1} e^{-\beta_i v}, 
\]
with weights \(p_i>0\) satisfying \(\sum_{i=1}^k p_i = 1\), and shapes \(\alpha_i>0\), 
rates \(\beta_i>0\).

Let \(g(\cdot)\) be a function that bounds the likelihood from above,
\[
f(y\mid v) \le g(v), \qquad v>0,
\]
and let \(c(v_0)\) be a subgradient of \(-\log g\) at a point \(v_0>0\),
\[
c(v_0) \in \partial[-\log g(v)]\Big|_{v=v_0}.
\]

Define the tilted density
\[
q(v)
= \frac{\exp\{-c(v_0)\,v\}\,\pi(v)}{\operatorname{MGF}_\pi(-c(v_0))},
\qquad v>0,
\]
where \(\operatorname{MGF}_\pi(t) = \mathbb{E}_\pi[e^{t v}]\).

Then:

1. The moment generating function at \(-c(v_0)\) is
   \[
   \operatorname{MGF}_\pi(-c(v_0))
   = \sum_{i=1}^k p_i 
     \left(\frac{\beta_i}{\beta_i + c(v_0)}\right)^{\alpha_i},
     \qquad c(v_0) > -\min_i \beta_i.
   \]

2. The generalized likelihood-subgradient density \(q(v)\) is also a finite
   mixture of Gamma distributions,
   \[
   q(v) = \sum_{i=1}^k \tilde{p}_i\,\tilde{\pi}_i(v),
   \]
   where each component \(\tilde{\pi}_i\) is Gamma with the **same shape** 
   and a **shifted rate**,
   \[
   \tilde{\pi}_i(v)
   = \frac{(\beta_i + c(v_0))^{\alpha_i}}{\Gamma(\alpha_i)}
     v^{\alpha_i - 1} e^{-(\beta_i + c(v_0)) v},
   \]
   and the updated mixture weights are
   \[
   \tilde{p}_i
   = \frac{p_i \left(\dfrac{\beta_i}{\beta_i + c(v_0)}\right)^{\alpha_i}}
           {\displaystyle
            \sum_{j=1}^k 
            p_j \left(\dfrac{\beta_j}{\beta_j + c(v_0)}\right)^{\alpha_j}},
   \qquad i=1,\dots,k.
   \]

In particular, exponential tilting by \(\exp\{-c(v_0)\,v\}\) preserves the Gamma
family within each mixture component and only shifts the rate parameters from
\(\beta_i\) to \(\beta_i + c(v_0)\), while the mixture weights are updated via the
component-wise factors \((\beta_i/(\beta_i + c(v_0)))^{\alpha_i}\).


### Theorem 1 (Envelope factorization for generalized likelihood-subgradient densities)

Let \(q(\cdot)\) be the generalized likelihood-subgradient density at a point \(v_{0}\), constructed from a prior
\(\pi(v)\), a bounding function \(g(v)\) satisfying \(f(y \mid v) \le g(v)\), and a subgradient
\(c(v_{0})\) of \(-\log g(v)\) at \(v_{0}\).

Define
\[
a(v_{0})
=
\frac{g(v_{0}) \, \operatorname{MGF}_{\pi}(-c(v_{0}))}
     {f(y)} ,
\]
and
\[
h_{v_{0}}(v)
=
\frac{\exp\{-c(v_{0})(v - v_{0})\} \, g(v)}
     {g(v_{0})}.
\]

Then the posterior density satisfies the factorization
\[
\pi(v \mid y)
=
a(v_{0}) \, h_{v_{0}}(v) \, q(v),
\]
and the remainder term obeys the bound
\[
0 \le h_{v_{0}}(v) \le 1
\qquad \text{for all } v>0.
\]

If the bounding function is chosen to be the likelihood itself,
\(g(v) = f(y \mid v)\),
then the envelope is exact at the anchor point:
\[
h_{v_{0}}(v_{0}) = 1.
\]

### Interpretation

The theorem shows that every likelihood-subgradient density produces a valid
accept-reject envelope for the posterior.  
The acceptance probability is \(1/a(v_{0})\), and the tightness of the envelope
is determined by the choice of the anchor point \(v_{0}\) and the subgradient
\(c(v_{0})\).  
For exponential-family priors (including Gamma mixtures), the tilted density
\(q(v)\) remains in the same family, making this construction especially useful
for dispersion sampling in Gamma regression.


## 1.2 Models with Normal Data - Examples

In section, we work throi

### Example 1 (Normal likelihood with Gamma prior on precision)

Let \(y_1,\dots,y_n\) be i.i.d. normal with known mean \(\mu\) and unknown precision
\(\lambda > 0\):
\[
y_i \mid \lambda \sim \mathcal{N}\!\left(\mu,\; \lambda^{-1}\right).
\]
Define the sufficient statistic
\[
S = \sum_{i=1}^n (y_i - \mu)^2.
\]

The likelihood for \(\lambda\) is
\[
f(y \mid \lambda)
= (2\pi)^{-n/2}\,\lambda^{n/2}
  \exp\!\left(-\frac{\lambda S}{2}\right),
\]
so the log-likelihood is
\[
\ell(\lambda)
= \log f(y \mid \lambda)
= -\frac{n}{2}\log(2\pi)
  + \frac{n}{2}\log\lambda
  - \frac{\lambda S}{2}.
\]

Assume a Gamma prior on the precision,
\[
\lambda \sim \mathrm{Gamma}(\alpha_0,\beta_0),
\qquad
\pi(\lambda)
= \frac{\beta_0^{\alpha_0}}{\Gamma(\alpha_0)}
  \lambda^{\alpha_0 - 1} e^{-\beta_0 \lambda},
\quad \lambda>0,
\]
with shape \(\alpha_0>0\) and rate \(\beta_0>0\).

The posterior is conjugate:
\[
\lambda \mid y \sim
\mathrm{Gamma}\!\left(\alpha_n,\beta_n\right),
\qquad
\alpha_n = \alpha_0 + \frac{n}{2},
\quad
\beta_n  = \beta_0 + \frac{S}{2},
\]
so the posterior density is
\[
\pi(\lambda \mid y)
= \frac{\beta_n^{\alpha_n}}{\Gamma(\alpha_n)}
  \lambda^{\alpha_n - 1} e^{-\beta_n \lambda},
\quad \lambda>0.
\]

We now construct a likelihood-subgradient density using the prior
\(\pi\) and the likelihood \(f(y\mid\lambda)\), following Definition 2 in
Nygren and Nygren (2006).

Take the bounding function \(g\) equal to the likelihood:
\[
g(\lambda) = f(y\mid\lambda),
\]
and let \(\lambda_0>0\) be a fixed anchor point. The negative log of the
bounding function is
\[
-\log g(\lambda)
= \frac{n}{2}\log(2\pi)
  - \frac{n}{2}\log\lambda
  + \frac{\lambda S}{2}.
\]
The derivative (subgradient in the univariate, smooth case) at
\(\lambda_0\) is
\[
c(\lambda_0)
= \frac{d}{d\lambda}\bigl[-\log g(\lambda)\bigr]_{\lambda=\lambda_0}
= -\frac{n}{2\lambda_0} + \frac{S}{2}.
\]

The corresponding likelihood-subgradient density is
\[
q(\lambda)
=
\frac{\exp\{-c(\lambda_0)\,\lambda\}\,\pi(\lambda)}
     {\operatorname{MGF}_\pi(-c(\lambda_0))},
\qquad \lambda>0,
\]
where
\[
\operatorname{MGF}_\pi(t)
= \mathbb{E}_\pi[e^{t\lambda}]
= \left(\frac{\beta_0}{\beta_0 - t}\right)^{\alpha_0},
\quad t < \beta_0.
\]
For \(t = -c(\lambda_0)\) we obtain
\[
\operatorname{MGF}_\pi(-c(\lambda_0))
= \left(\frac{\beta_0}{\beta_0 + c(\lambda_0)}\right)^{\alpha_0},
\qquad
\beta_0 + c(\lambda_0) > 0.
\]

Because the prior is Gamma, the likelihood-subgradient density is also
Gamma, with the same shape and a shifted rate:
\[
q(\lambda)
= \mathrm{Gamma}\bigl(\alpha_0,\;\beta_0 + c(\lambda_0)\bigr),
\]
i.e.
\[
q(\lambda)
= \frac{(\beta_0 + c(\lambda_0))^{\alpha_0}}{\Gamma(\alpha_0)}
  \lambda^{\alpha_0 - 1}
  \exp\!\bigl(-(\beta_0 + c(\lambda_0))\lambda\bigr).
\]

To study the efficiency of the resulting envelope, we define the
accept–reject constant \(a(\lambda_0)\) in analogy with equation (6) in
Nygren and Nygren (2006). From Theorem 1 in that paper,
\[
a(\lambda_0)
=
\frac{g(\lambda_0)\operatorname{MGF}_\pi(-c(\lambda_0))}
     {f(y)\,\exp\{-c(\lambda_0)\lambda_0\}},
\]
where \(f(y)\) is the marginal likelihood,
\[
f(y)
=
\int_0^\infty f(y\mid\lambda)\,\pi(\lambda)\,d\lambda.
\]

For this normal–Gamma model, the marginal likelihood is available in
closed form:
\[
f(y)
=
(2\pi)^{-n/2}
\frac{\beta_0^{\alpha_0}}{\Gamma(\alpha_0)}
\frac{\Gamma(\alpha_n)}{\beta_n^{\alpha_n}}.
\]

Substituting
\[
g(\lambda_0) = f(y\mid\lambda_0)
= (2\pi)^{-n/2}\lambda_0^{n/2}\exp\!\left(-\frac{\lambda_0 S}{2}\right),
\]
\[
\operatorname{MGF}_\pi(-c(\lambda_0))
=
\left(\frac{\beta_0}{\beta_0 + c(\lambda_0)}\right)^{\alpha_0},
\]
and
\[
\exp\{-c(\lambda_0)\lambda_0\}
=
\exp\!\left\{
-\left(-\frac{n}{2\lambda_0} + \frac{S}{2}\right)\lambda_0
\right\}
=
\exp\!\left(-\frac{n}{2} + \frac{\lambda_0 S}{2}\right),
\]
into the expression for \(a(\lambda_0)\), we obtain
\[
a(\lambda_0)
=
\frac{
 (2\pi)^{-n/2}\lambda_0^{n/2}
 \exp\!\left(-\frac{\lambda_0 S}{2}\right)
 \left(\dfrac{\beta_0}{\beta_0 + c(\lambda_0)}\right)^{\alpha_0}
}{
 (2\pi)^{-n/2}
 \dfrac{\beta_0^{\alpha_0}}{\Gamma(\alpha_0)}
 \dfrac{\Gamma(\alpha_n)}{\beta_n^{\alpha_n}}
 \exp\!\left(-\frac{n}{2} + \frac{\lambda_0 S}{2}\right)
}.
\]

The \((2\pi)^{-n/2}\) terms cancel, and the exponential terms
\(\exp(-\lambda_0 S/2)\) and \(\exp(\lambda_0 S/2)\) combine to
\(\exp(-n/2)\), yielding the explicit envelope constant
\[
a(\lambda_0)
=
\exp\!\left(-\frac{n}{2}\right)
\lambda_0^{n/2}
\left(\frac{\beta_0}{\beta_0 + c(\lambda_0)}\right)^{\alpha_0}
\frac{\Gamma(\alpha_0)\,\beta_n^{\alpha_n}}{\beta_0^{\alpha_0}\,\Gamma(\alpha_n)},
\]
with
\[
c(\lambda_0)
= -\frac{n}{2\lambda_0} + \frac{S}{2},
\qquad
\alpha_n = \alpha_0 + \frac{n}{2},
\quad
\beta_n  = \beta_0 + \frac{S}{2}.
\]

This scalar expression plays the same role for the Gamma–precision
model as equation (7) does for the multivariate normal example in
Nygren and Nygren (2006): it quantifies the tightness of the envelope
resulting from a single likelihood-subgradient density anchored at
\(\lambda_0\), and it can be minimized over \(\lambda_0\) to obtain the
“best” single-tangent envelope.

In particular, differentiating \(\log a(\lambda_0)\) with respect to
\(\lambda_0\) shows that the minimizing value of \(\lambda_0\) coincides
with the posterior mode of \(\lambda\), mirroring the optimality
property of \(\theta^*\) in Example 1 of Nygren and Nygren (2006).
As in that example, the optimal \(a(\lambda_0)\) deteriorates as \(n\)
increases, illustrating the limitations of a single-tangent envelope and
motivating mixture constructions in more complex models.


##

### Posterior mode (or mean) and minimization of \(a(\lambda_0)\)

Recall from the example:

- Prior:
  \[
  \lambda \sim \mathrm{Gamma}(\alpha_0,\beta_0),
  \qquad
  \pi(\lambda)
  = \frac{\beta_0^{\alpha_0}}{\Gamma(\alpha_0)}
    \lambda^{\alpha_0 - 1} e^{-\beta_0 \lambda}.
  \]
- Likelihood:
  \[
  f(y \mid \lambda)
  = (2\pi)^{-n/2}\,\lambda^{n/2}
    \exp\!\left(-\frac{\lambda S}{2}\right),
  \qquad
  S = \sum_{i=1}^n (y_i - \mu)^2.
  \]
- Posterior:
  \[
  \lambda \mid y \sim
  \mathrm{Gamma}(\alpha_n,\beta_n),
  \qquad
  \alpha_n = \alpha_0 + \frac{n}{2},
  \quad
  \beta_n  = \beta_0 + \frac{S}{2}.
  \]

The negative log of the bounding function \(g(\lambda)=f(y\mid\lambda)\) is
\[
-\log g(\lambda)
= \frac{n}{2}\log(2\pi)
  - \frac{n}{2}\log\lambda
  + \frac{\lambda S}{2},
\]
so the subgradient at \(\lambda_0>0\) is
\[
c(\lambda_0)
= \frac{d}{d\lambda}\bigl[-\log g(\lambda)\bigr]_{\lambda=\lambda_0}
= -\frac{n}{2\lambda_0} + \frac{S}{2}.
\]

The envelope constant from Theorem 1 of Nygren & Nygren (2006) is
\[
a(\lambda_0)
=
\frac{g(\lambda_0)\operatorname{MGF}_\pi(-c(\lambda_0))}
     {f(y)\,\exp\{-c(\lambda_0)\lambda_0\}},
\]
where
\[
g(\lambda_0) = f(y\mid\lambda_0)
= (2\pi)^{-n/2}\lambda_0^{n/2}
  \exp\!\left(-\frac{\lambda_0 S}{2}\right),
\]
\[
\operatorname{MGF}_\pi(-c(\lambda_0))
=
\left(\frac{\beta_0}{\beta_0 + c(\lambda_0)}\right)^{\alpha_0},
\qquad
\beta_0 + c(\lambda_0) > 0,
\]
and the marginal likelihood is
\[
f(y)
=
(2\pi)^{-n/2}
\frac{\beta_0^{\alpha_0}}{\Gamma(\alpha_0)}
\frac{\Gamma(\alpha_n)}{\beta_n^{\alpha_n}}.
\]

Moreover,
\[
\exp\{-c(\lambda_0)\lambda_0\}
=
\exp\!\left\{
-\left(-\frac{n}{2\lambda_0} + \frac{S}{2}\right)\lambda_0
\right\}
=
\exp\!\left(-\frac{n}{2} + \frac{\lambda_0 S}{2}\right).
\]

Putting these together and cancelling the \((2\pi)^{-n/2}\) factors yields
\[
a(\lambda_0)
=
\exp\!\left(-\frac{n}{2}\right)
\lambda_0^{n/2}
\left(\frac{\beta_0}{\beta_0 + c(\lambda_0)}\right)^{\alpha_0}
\frac{\Gamma(\alpha_0)\,\beta_n^{\alpha_n}}{\beta_0^{\alpha_0}\,\Gamma(\alpha_n)},
\]
with
\[
c(\lambda_0)
= -\frac{n}{2\lambda_0} + \frac{S}{2},
\qquad
\alpha_n = \alpha_0 + \frac{n}{2},
\quad
\beta_n  = \beta_0 + \frac{S}{2}.
\]

For optimization it is convenient to note that
\[
\beta_0 + c(\lambda_0)
= \beta_0 + \frac{S}{2} - \frac{n}{2\lambda_0}
= \beta_n - \frac{n}{2\lambda_0}.
\]

Ignoring multiplicative constants that do not depend on \(\lambda_0\),
we can write
\[
\log a(\lambda_0)
= \frac{n}{2}\log\lambda_0
  + \alpha_0\bigl[\log\beta_0 - \log(\beta_n - \tfrac{n}{2\lambda_0})\bigr]
  + \text{const},
\]
where "const" does not depend on \(\lambda_0\).

Differentiate with respect to \(\lambda_0\):
\[
\frac{d}{d\lambda_0}\log a(\lambda_0)
= \frac{n}{2}\cdot\frac{1}{\lambda_0}
  - \alpha_0\,
    \frac{\tfrac{n}{2\lambda_0^2}}{\beta_n - \tfrac{n}{2\lambda_0}}.
\]

Set this derivative equal to zero to find the minimizer:
\[
\frac{n}{2}\cdot\frac{1}{\lambda_0}
=
\alpha_0\,
\frac{\tfrac{n}{2\lambda_0^2}}{\beta_n - \tfrac{n}{2\lambda_0}}.
\]

Cancel the common factor \(n/2\) and multiply both sides by \(\lambda_0^2\):
\[
\lambda_0
=
\frac{\alpha_0}{\beta_n - \tfrac{n}{2\lambda_0}}.
\]

Rearrange:
\[
\lambda_0\left(\beta_n - \frac{n}{2\lambda_0}\right)
= \alpha_0
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
\lambda_0\beta_n - \frac{n}{2}
= \alpha_0.
\]

Hence the unique stationary point is
\[
\lambda_0^{\star}
= \frac{\alpha_0 + \frac{n}{2}}{\beta_n}
= \frac{\alpha_n}{\beta_n}.
\]

Because the second derivative of \(\log a(\lambda_0)\) at this point is
negative (for \(\alpha_0>0\), \(\beta_n>0\)), this stationary point
is the unique minimizer of \(a(\lambda_0)\).

Thus, in this normal–Gamma precision example, the envelope constant
\(a(\lambda_0)\) is minimized when the anchor point \(\lambda_0\) is set
equal to the **posterior mean**
\[
\mathbb{E}[\lambda\mid y] = \frac{\alpha_n}{\beta_n},
\]
rather than the posterior mode \((\alpha_n-1)/\beta_n\).
For large \(n\), however, the posterior mean and mode are asymptotically
close, so the optimal \(\lambda_0^{\star}\) and the posterior mode coincide
up to \(O(1/n)\) terms, mirroring the behavior of the multivariate normal
example in Nygren and Nygren (2006).

Substituting \(\lambda_0^{\star} = \alpha_n/\beta_n\) into the expression
for \(a(\lambda_0)\), we obtain
\[
\beta_0 + c(\lambda_0^{\star})
= \beta_n - \frac{n}{2\lambda_0^{\star}}
= \beta_n - \frac{n}{2}\cdot\frac{\beta_n}{\alpha_n}
= \beta_n\left(1 - \frac{n}{2\alpha_n}\right)
= \beta_n\frac{2\alpha_n - n}{2\alpha_n}
= \beta_n\frac{2\alpha_0}{2\alpha_n}
= \beta_n\frac{\alpha_0}{\alpha_n},
\]
so that
\[
\frac{\beta_0}{\beta_0 + c(\lambda_0^{\star})}
=
\frac{\beta_0}{\beta_n\,\alpha_0/\alpha_n}
=
\frac{\beta_0\alpha_n}{\beta_n\alpha_0},
\]
and
\[
\left(\frac{\beta_0}{\beta_0 + c(\lambda_0^{\star})}\right)^{\alpha_0}
=
\left(\frac{\beta_0\alpha_n}{\beta_n\alpha_0}\right)^{\alpha_0}.
\]

Thus the optimized envelope constant simplifies to
\[
a(\lambda_0^{\star})
=
\exp\!\left(-\frac{n}{2}\right)
\left(\frac{\alpha_n}{\beta_n}\right)^{n/2}
\left(\frac{\beta_0\alpha_n}{\beta_n\alpha_0}\right)^{\alpha_0}
\frac{\Gamma(\alpha_0)\,\beta_n^{\alpha_n}}{\beta_0^{\alpha_0}\,\Gamma(\alpha_n)}.
\]

This explicit form plays the same role here as equation (7) does in
Example 1 of Nygren and Nygren (2006): it quantifies the tightness of
the best single-tangent envelope in this normal–Gamma precision setting
and makes the deterioration of \(a(\lambda_0^{\star})\) with increasing
sample size \(n\) analytically transparent.

<!-- ### Special case: \(\beta_0/\alpha_0 = \beta_n/\alpha_n = S/n\) -->

<!-- Assume -->
<!-- \[ -->
<!-- \frac{\beta_0}{\alpha_0} = \frac{\beta_n}{\alpha_n} = \frac{S}{n} = c, -->
<!-- \quad S/n \text{ fixed in } n. -->
<!-- \] -->
<!-- Then -->
<!-- \[ -->
<!-- \beta_0 = c\,\alpha_0,\qquad -->
<!-- \beta_n = c\,\alpha_n, -->
<!-- \qquad -->
<!-- \alpha_n = \alpha_0 + \frac{n}{2}. -->
<!-- \] -->

<!-- The optimized envelope constant is -->
<!-- \[ -->
<!-- a(\lambda_0^{\star}) -->
<!-- = -->
<!-- \exp\!\left(-\frac{n}{2}\right) -->
<!-- \left(\frac{\alpha_n}{\beta_n}\right)^{n/2} -->
<!-- \left(\frac{\beta_0\alpha_n}{\beta_n\alpha_0}\right)^{\alpha_0} -->
<!-- \frac{\Gamma(\alpha_0)\,\beta_n^{\alpha_n}}{\beta_0^{\alpha_0}\,\Gamma(\alpha_n)}. -->
<!-- \] -->

<!-- Under the special case: -->

<!-- 1. **Second factor**: -->
<!--    \[ -->
<!--    \frac{\alpha_n}{\beta_n} -->
<!--    = \frac{\alpha_n}{c\,\alpha_n} -->
<!--    = \frac{1}{c}, -->
<!--    \] -->
<!--    so -->
<!--    \[ -->
<!--    \left(\frac{\alpha_n}{\beta_n}\right)^{n/2} -->
<!--    = \left(\frac{1}{c}\right)^{n/2}, -->
<!--    \] -->
<!--    which is *independent of* \(\alpha_n,\beta_n\) (and thus independent of how the posterior updates), but still depends on \(n\) through the exponent. -->

<!-- 2. **Third factor**: -->
<!--    \[ -->
<!--    \frac{\beta_0\alpha_n}{\beta_n\alpha_0} -->
<!--    = \frac{c\,\alpha_0\,\alpha_n}{c\,\alpha_n\,\alpha_0} -->
<!--    = 1, -->
<!--    \] -->
<!--    hence -->
<!--    \[ -->
<!--    \left(\frac{\beta_0\alpha_n}{\beta_n\alpha_0}\right)^{\alpha_0} = 1. -->
<!--    \] -->

<!-- So under your calibration, the expression simplifies to -->
<!-- \[ -->
<!-- a(\lambda_0^{\star}) -->
<!-- = -->
<!-- \exp\!\left(-\frac{n}{2}\right) -->
<!-- \left(\frac{1}{c}\right)^{n/2} -->
<!-- \frac{\Gamma(\alpha_0)\,(c\,\alpha_n)^{\alpha_n}}{(c\,\alpha_0)^{\alpha_0}\,\Gamma(\alpha_n)}. -->
<!-- \] -->

<!-- Equivalently, -->
<!-- \[ -->
<!-- a(\lambda_0^{\star}) -->
<!-- = -->
<!-- \exp\!\left(-\frac{n}{2}\right) -->
<!-- \left(\frac{1}{c}\right)^{n/2} -->
<!-- \cdot -->
<!-- \frac{\Gamma(\alpha_0)}{\Gamma(\alpha_n)} -->
<!-- \cdot -->
<!-- c^{\alpha_n-\alpha_0}\, -->
<!-- \frac{\alpha_n^{\alpha_n}}{\alpha_0^{\alpha_0}}. -->
<!-- \] -->

<!-- You’re exactly right that, in this calibrated case, the “shape” of the behavior is driven by -->

<!-- - the exponential term \(\exp(-n/2)\),   -->
<!-- - the Stirling-type behavior of \(\Gamma(\alpha_n)\),   -->
<!-- - and the \(\alpha_n^{\alpha_n}\) factor, -->

<!-- rather than by any drift in \(\beta_n/\alpha_n\), which has been pinned down to equal \(S/n\). -->


<!-- ### Special case analysis: \(\beta_0/\alpha_0 = \beta_n/\alpha_n = S/n\) -->

<!-- Your instinct to look at the ratio \(\beta_n/\alpha_n\) is exactly right; it’s the quantity that behaves like an “effective variance” in this precision story. -->

<!-- Recall: -->
<!-- \[ -->
<!-- \alpha_n = \alpha_0 + \frac{n}{2}, -->
<!-- \qquad -->
<!-- \beta_n  = \beta_0 + \frac{S}{2}. -->
<!-- \] -->

<!-- Impose the special case -->
<!-- \[ -->
<!-- \frac{\beta_0}{\alpha_0} = \frac{\beta_n}{\alpha_n} = \frac{S}{n} =: c, -->
<!-- \] -->
<!-- with \(c>0\) fixed and \(S/n\) constant in \(n\). -->

<!-- Then: -->

<!-- 1. From \(\beta_0/\alpha_0 = c\), we get -->
<!--    \[ -->
<!--    \beta_0 = c\,\alpha_0. -->
<!--    \] -->
<!-- 2. From \(\beta_n/\alpha_n = c\), we get -->
<!--    \[ -->
<!--    \beta_n = c\,\alpha_n. -->
<!--    \] -->
<!-- 3. From the definition of \(\beta_n\), -->
<!--    \[ -->
<!--    \beta_n = \beta_0 + \frac{S}{2} -->
<!--    \quad\Longrightarrow\quad -->
<!--    c\,\alpha_n = c\,\alpha_0 + \frac{c n}{2}, -->
<!--    \] -->
<!--    because \(S = cn\). But \(\alpha_n = \alpha_0 + n/2\), so this identity is automatically satisfied. Thus the constraint is internally consistent for all \(n\), given \(\beta_0 = c\,\alpha_0\). -->

<!-- Under this special case, the posterior mean -->
<!-- \[ -->
<!-- \lambda_0^{*} = \frac{\alpha_{n}}{\beta_{n}} = \frac{1}{c} -->
<!-- \] -->
<!-- is **constant in \(n\)**, which matches your intuition: the “target precision” stays fixed, so the optimized anchor \(\lambda_0^{*} \) does not move as you add more data. -->

<!-- --- -->

<!-- ### Behavior of \(a(\lambda_0^{*} )\) in this special case -->

<!-- From the earlier derivation (which didn’t use any assumption on \(\beta_n/\alpha_n\)), we already simplified the optimized envelope constant to -->
<!-- \[ -->
<!-- \log a(\lambda_0^{*} ) -->
<!-- = -->
<!-- -\frac{n}{2} -->
<!-- + \Bigl(\frac{n}{2} + \alpha_0\Bigr)\log \alpha_n -->
<!-- - \log \Gamma(\alpha_n) -->
<!-- + \text{const}, -->
<!-- \] -->
<!-- with \(\alpha_n = \alpha_0 + n/2\). Under the special case, nothing in this expression changes algebraically: the \(\log\beta_n\) terms have already cancelled, so the \(\beta_n/\alpha_n\) constraint doesn’t further alter the formula. -->

<!-- Hence the large-\(n\) asymptotics are governed by the balance between -->

<!-- - the linear term \(-n/2\), -->
<!-- - the “entropy” term \(\log\Gamma(\alpha_n)\), -->
<!-- - and the \((n/2+\alpha_0)\log\alpha_n\) term. -->

<!-- Using Stirling again, -->
<!-- \[ -->
<!-- \log \Gamma(\alpha_n) -->
<!-- = -->
<!-- \Bigl(\alpha_n - \tfrac{1}{2}\Bigr)\log \alpha_n -->
<!-- - \alpha_n -->
<!-- + O(1), -->
<!-- \] -->
<!-- so -->
<!-- \[ -->
<!-- \log a(\lambda_0^{*}  ) -->
<!-- = -->
<!-- -\frac{n}{2} -->
<!-- + \Bigl(\frac{n}{2} + \alpha_0\Bigr)\log \alpha_n -->
<!-- - \Bigl(\alpha_n - \tfrac{1}{2}\Bigr)\log \alpha_n -->
<!-- + \alpha_n -->
<!-- + O(1). -->
<!-- \] -->

<!-- As before, the coefficient of \(\log\alpha_n\) is -->
<!-- \[ -->
<!-- \frac{n}{2} + \alpha_0 - \Bigl(\alpha_0 + \frac{n}{2} - \frac{1}{2}\Bigr) -->
<!-- = \frac{1}{2}, -->
<!-- \] -->
<!-- and \(\alpha_n = \alpha_0 + n/2\) gives -->
<!-- \[ -->
<!-- \log a(\lambda_0^{*}) -->
<!-- = -->
<!-- -\frac{n}{2} -->
<!-- + \frac{1}{2}\log(\alpha_0 + n/2) -->
<!-- + \alpha_0 + \frac{n}{2} -->
<!-- + O(1) -->
<!-- = -->
<!-- \alpha_0 + \frac{1}{2}\log(\alpha_0 + n/2) + O(1). -->
<!-- \] -->

<!-- Thus, even in the special case where -->
<!-- \[ -->
<!-- \frac{\beta_0}{\alpha_0} -->
<!-- = \frac{\beta_n}{\alpha_n} -->
<!-- = \frac{S}{n} -->
<!-- = c, -->
<!-- \] -->
<!-- and the anchor \(\lambda_0^{*}\) is fixed, we still get the same asymptotic behavior: -->
<!-- \[ -->
<!-- \log a(\lambda_0^{*} ) = \frac{1}{2}\log n + O(1), -->
<!-- \qquad -->
<!-- a(\lambda_0^{*}) \asymp \text{const}\cdot n^{1/2}. -->
<!-- \] -->

<!-- So you’re exactly right that: -->

<!-- - **You cannot infer the asymptotics from any single multiplicative factor** like \(\exp(-n/2)\); it’s the cancellation pattern—particularly with \(\log\Gamma(\alpha_n)\)—that determines the rate. -->
<!-- - In this carefully calibrated “constant \(\beta_n/\alpha_n\)” regime, the optimized single-tangent envelope still deteriorates, but only at a **\(\sqrt{n}\)** rate, and that conclusion only appears after tracking each term in \(\log a(\lambda_0^{*})\) and using Stirling. -->

<!-- What your special case buys you is conceptual clarity: it decouples the “effective precision level” (fixed via \(\beta_n/\alpha_n\)) from the pure sample-size effect, making it transparent that the growth in \(a(\lambda_0^{*})\) is driven entirely by the combinatorial/entropy structure of the Gamma shape parameter, not by drift in the posterior mean precision itself. -->

##

## Gradient of the log‑posterior \( f'(v) \) for the Gamma–GLM precision

For observations  
\[
Y_i \mid v \sim \text{Gamma}(\text{shape}=v,\ \text{rate}=v/\mu_i),
\qquad 
\mu_i = \exp(x_i^\top\beta + \alpha_i),
\]
with weights \(w_i\), and prior  
\[
v \sim \text{Gamma}(a_0, b_0),
\]
the unnormalized log‑posterior is
\[
f(v)
=
\sum_{i=1}^n
w_i\Big[
v\log v
- v\log\mu_i
+ (v-1)\log y_i
- \log\Gamma(v)
- v\,\frac{y_i}{\mu_i}
\Big]
+
(a_0 - 1)\log v
- b_0 v.
\]

Differentiating term by term with respect to \(v\), the gradient of the log‑posterior is
\[
\boxed{
f'(v)
=
\sum_{i=1}^n
w_i\Big[
\log v + 1
- \log\mu_i
+ \log y_i
- \psi(v)
- \frac{y_i}{\mu_i}
\Big]
+
\frac{a_0 - 1}{v}
- b_0
}
\]
where \( \psi(v) \) is the digamma function.
###



\[
f'(v)
=
\underbrace{
\left(\sum_{i=1}^n w_i\right)\big[\log v - \psi(v)\big]
+
\frac{a_0 - 1}{v}
}_{\text{terms depending on } v}
\;+\;
\underbrace{
\sum_{i=1}^n w_i\Big[1 - \log\mu_i + \log y_i - \frac{y_i}{\mu_i}\Big]
- b_0
}_{\text{terms not depending on } v}
\]


\[
\boxed{
b_{\text{post}}(v)
=
b_0
+
\sum_{i=1}^n
w_i\Big[
\frac{y_i}{\mu_i}
+ \log\mu_i
- \log y_i
- 1
\Big]
}
\]

where the sum is guaranteed to be non-negative.

## Second derivative of the log‑posterior \( f''(v) \) for the Gamma–GLM precision

Recall the gradient for the full log‑posterior
\[
f'(v)
=
\sum_{i=1}^n
w_i\Big[
\log v + 1
- \log\mu_i
+ \log y_i
- \psi(v)
- \frac{y_i}{\mu_i}
\Big]
+
\frac{a_0 - 1}{v}
- b_0,
\]
where \( \psi(v) \) is the digamma function.

Differentiate term by term with respect to \(v\).

- For the likelihood part inside the sum:
  - \( \dfrac{d}{dv}(\log v) = \dfrac{1}{v} \)
  - \( \dfrac{d}{dv}(1) = 0 \)
  - \( \dfrac{d}{dv}(-\log\mu_i) = 0 \) (since \(\mu_i\) is fixed here)
  - \( \dfrac{d}{dv}(\log y_i) = 0 \) (data)
  - \( \dfrac{d}{dv}[-\psi(v)] = -\psi_1(v) \), where \( \psi_1(v) \) is the trigamma function
  - \( \dfrac{d}{dv}\Big(-\dfrac{y_i}{\mu_i}\Big) = 0 \) (since \(y_i,\mu_i\) are fixed)

So for each \(i\),
\[
\frac{d}{dv}
\Big[
\log v + 1
- \log\mu_i
+ \log y_i
- \psi(v)
- \frac{y_i}{\mu_i}
\Big]
=
\frac{1}{v} - \psi_1(v).
\]

Multiplying by \(w_i\) and summing over \(i\),
\[
\frac{d}{dv}
\left(
\sum_{i=1}^n
w_i\Big[
\log v + 1
- \log\mu_i
+ \log y_i
- \psi(v)
- \frac{y_i}{\mu_i}
\Big]
\right)
=
\sum_{i=1}^n
w_i\Big(
\frac{1}{v} - \psi_1(v)
\Big).
\]

- For the prior term:
  - \( \dfrac{d}{dv}\left(\dfrac{a_0 - 1}{v}\right) = -\dfrac{a_0 - 1}{v^2} \)
  - \( \dfrac{d}{dv}(-b_0) = 0 \)

Putting it all together,
\[
f''(v)
=
\sum_{i=1}^n
w_i\Big(
\frac{1}{v} - \psi_1(v)
\Big)
-
\frac{a_0 - 1}{v^2}.
\]

Equivalently,
\[
\boxed{
f''(v)
=
\left(\sum_{i=1}^n w_i\right)\frac{1}{v}
-
\left(\sum_{i=1}^n w_i\right)\psi_1(v)
-
\frac{a_0 - 1}{v^2}
}
\]

and, after putting everything over a common denominator \(v^2\),
\[
\boxed{
f''(v)
=
\frac{
\left(\sum_{i=1}^n w_i\right)v
-
\left(\sum_{i=1}^n w_i\right)\psi_1(v)\,v^2
-
(a_0 - 1)
}{
v^2
}
}
\]
where \( \psi_1(v) \) is the trigamma function.

This motivates defining the curvature‑based quantity
\[
a_{\text{post}}(v)
=
a_0
+
\left(\sum_{i=1}^n w_i\right)\psi_1(v)\,v^2
-
\left(\sum_{i=1}^n w_i\right)v,
\]
so that it satisfies the identity
\[
a_{\text{post}}(v) - 1
=
-\,v^2\, f''(v).
\]


In the above results, we noted that the optimal positioning of the tangency point is at the expected value for the precision under the posterior. To derive an estimate for this
